items.question: Who benefits from consistent metric tracking in ML experiments?
items.answer: ML engineers and data scientists compare runs and debug training; team leads read validation curves for go/no-go calls; downstream MLOps uses locked test metrics for release records— everyone needs the same split-prefixed names across runs.

items.question: What is tracking metrics in an ML experiment?
items.answer: Recording numeric evaluation results— loss, accuracy, F1, RMSE, and related scalars— over steps or epochs, tagged by data split (train, validation, test) and stable metric names so runs and curves are comparable.

items.question: When should you log validation vs test metrics during training?
items.answer: Log validation metrics every epoch or evaluation pass for model selection and early stopping; log test metrics once at sign-off after the best checkpoint is chosen— not every epoch as a live tuning dashboard.

items.question: Where do split-prefixed metric names appear in a training loop?
items.answer: Inside the training loop and eval hooks: train/* after forward/backward on training batches, val/* after a validation pass on held-out data, test/* in a separate final evaluation script— each written to the experiment log or tracker.

items.question: Why use train/ and val/ prefixes instead of generic names like loss?
items.answer: Unprefixed loss conflates splits— you cannot plot train vs validation on one chart or compare runs fairly. Split prefixes make overfitting visible when train/loss falls while val/loss rises.

items.question: How do you log comparable scalar curves across hyperparameter sweeps?
items.answer: Use identical metric keys (e.g. val/f1, val/loss) and the same x-axis unit (epoch or global_step) in every run; vary only hyperparameters and run metadata so the tracker overlays curves without renaming.

items.question: train/loss vs val/loss — what does each measure?
items.answer: train/loss is the optimization objective on batches seen during fit()— it measures fit on training data. val/loss is the same loss function on held-out validation rows— it estimates generalization during development and drives model selection.

items.question: Per-step logging vs per-epoch logging — when does each apply?
items.answer: Per-step logging suits long epochs and deep models where instability appears within an epoch— train/loss updates frequently, val/* less often. Per-epoch logging suits classical full-pass training where one validation pass per epoch is enough for a smooth selection curve.

items.question: Cause: test/f1 is logged every epoch and used to pick the best checkpoint. Effect?
items.answer: The test set participates in model selection through the metric log— reported test/f1 is optimistically biased and no longer an honest final generalization estimate, mirroring test-set tuning leakage.

items.question: Cause: runs use different metric keys (run A logs val_f1, run B logs val/f1). Effect?
items.answer: Experiment comparison breaks— dashboards and leaderboards cannot align curves or rank runs because the same quantity has incompatible names across experiments.
